Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings to listen to lecture recordings since that's how I learn best, but life kept getting in the way. I'm involved in a few extracurriculars, and between those commitments and just feeling overwhelmed, I'd often end up cramming things together at the last minute. I aimed for consistency but rarely achieved it.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was just too anxious to even start. Then when deadlines loomed, I'd have these intense bursts where I'd try to catch up on everything at once. It wasn't healthy, but that's how it went for me most of the semester.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification that scared me into action or a message from one of my peer study group members. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just scrolling around without really engaging. The peer support system we had actually helped motivate me to check in more than I would have on my own.
Q4: For a brief period mid-semester, logging in became somewhat routine because my peer support group had set up regular check-ins. We'd remind each other to watch certain videos or discuss topics together. But when my anxiety spiked and I started falling behind, that routine completely fell apart. It's hard to maintain habits when you're constantly feeling like you're drowning.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I'm an auditory learner and I could listen to them multiple times. I also found the discussion forums useful when my peers were active because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click. The peer interactions were probably what kept me somewhat connected to the course even when I was struggling to keep up with everything else.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and a lot of the supplementary PDFs. They just felt like walls of text that I couldn't process when I was already stressed. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. When you're anxious and bored at the same time, dense written content is the last thing you want to tackle.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams, or when it involved interacting with other students. If something felt isolated or abstract, I'd lose interest fast. I needed that sense of purpose or social connection to push through the boredom and anxiety that kept pulling me away from studying.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already behind on required work. There was this constant guilt about not doing the mandatory stuff, so adding optional things felt impossible. Sometimes I'd look at them and think they seemed interesting, but then the anxiety would kick in reminding me of all the assignments I hadn't completed yet.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of audio lectures and would listen to them on my commute or while doing other things. I also used some external YouTube videos that explained concepts in different ways. My peers would share notes and resources through our group chat that never showed up in the LMS. A lot of my actual learning happened outside that system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be discussing course concepts with my peer group over coffee or messaging, working through problems together, but none of that registered anywhere official. I'd also replay downloaded lectures while exercising or doing chores. The LMS only saw me when I actively logged in, but learning was happening in all these invisible moments throughout my day.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd download a video or save lecture slides once, then work with them offline for days. My internet at home is decent but I preferred having materials accessible without needing to log in every time. It was easier to study when I didn't have to face the LMS dashboard reminding me of everything I was behind on.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed for me emotionally. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started avoiding the course entirely because logging in made me feel worse. I was also dealing with some personal stuff and my extracurricular commitments were demanding more time. The boredom with the material mixed with high stress created this paralysis where I couldn't engage even when I wanted to.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from other responsibilities, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid because it represented all my failures. Confusion about certain topics made me feel stupid, which fed into my anxiety. I'd sometimes open the platform, see how much I'd missed, and just close it immediately because I couldn't handle the overwhelm.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd log in, click through some videos, maybe skim some content, but my brain wasn't actually absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about deadlines or just zoning out because the material felt tedious in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were piling up. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like, where do you even begin when you're already so far behind? I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It was this vicious cycle of avoidance that I couldn't break out of.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these panic-fueled sessions right before things were due. The LMS would show all my activity clustered around deadline dates because that's when desperation overcame my anxiety. It wasn't a healthy pattern, but deadlines were the external pressure I needed to act.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I often avoided looking at it because I was scared of what it would say. Positive feedback from peers in our support group motivated me more than official grades. The few times I got encouraging comments from instructors, it gave me a temporary boost, but that feeling faded quickly when stress took over again.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming to me. Having seven online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this one didn't feel like a priority when others had stricter attendance requirements. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually made it easier for me to push this course aside. I needed more structure, honestly, not less.
Q19: The peer support intervention actually made engagement easier—having people to connect with helped a lot. But the sheer volume of content and the way assignments were spread out made it hard to maintain momentum. Also, when I fell behind, there was no easy way to catch up, which made the gap feel insurmountable and killed my motivation further.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. You can spend hours clicking around, watching videos, participating in discussions, but if you're too anxious or confused to actually complete assignments, none of that activity translates to submitted work. Sometimes being active is about trying to understand rather than producing output. The engagement is real, but the deliverables don't follow.
Q21: I've seen classmates who barely log in but study effectively offline and ace exams. They probably have better focus and less anxiety than me. For me, low LMS activity meant low engagement overall, but I can see how someone with good self-discipline could learn everything they need from downloaded materials and show up just for assessments.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or how you were feeling while doing it. They miss all the offline studying, the peer conversations, the mental struggles. They see quantity but not quality. My logs probably look like sporadic panic rather than the genuine effort I was making in other ways.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the course somehow—whether through the material, the instructor, or other students. It's not just showing up; it's being mentally present and wanting to understand. For me, the peer connections were what engagement felt like, even when I was struggling with everything else.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting against my anxiety just to do the bare minimum. It can't show the hours I spent with my peer group working through concepts or the lectures I listened to while walking to clear my head. It definitely can't show that despite failing, I actually learned things and I'm not giving up. I'm still climbing, just slower than the system can measure.
